      "heading": "## 1. Title and Executive Summary",
      "content": [
        "This report proposes a control-informed research and system design for 3D reconstruction and phenotypic analysis of crop grains, with representative targets including wheat, maize, rice, soybean, sorghum, and similar seeds. The central design argument is that grain phenotyping should not be treated as a passive image-processing pipeline alone; it should be modeled as a controlled sensing-estimation-decision system in which sensor pose, illumination, exposure, conveyor or turntable motion, sensor scheduling, reconstruction uncertainty, segmentation confidence, and trait-estimation error are actively managed.",

A feedback-capable system can measure current uncertainty, identify which trait is under-observed, and command a new view, lighting pattern, turntable angle, conveyor stop, or CT-reference route before reporting a phenotype [[28]](https://www.dcsc.tudelft.nl/~sc4081/2018/assign/pap/andrea_ASR_paper.pdf), [[37]](https://rpg.ifi.uzh.ch/docs/ICRA16_Isler.pdf).",

        "CT and micro-CT are strongest when internal 3D traits are required.       "heading": "## 6. Problem Formulation and System Objectives",

      "heading": "## 7. Modern Control Theory Framework for the Research Area",
      "content": [
        "Modern control theory is central because the grain phenotyping platform is a dynamic system with actuated sensing. The plant, in control terms, is the combined sample-handling, imaging, illumination, reconstruction, and decision process. The system can be represented as x_{k+1}=f(x_k,u_k,w_k), y_k=h(x_k,u_k,v_k), with a belief state p(x_k | y_{1:k}, u_{1:k}) updated after each observation and a policy u_k=mu(p_k,r_k,c_k) selecting the next action.",
        "Observability asks whether full geometry, pose, reflectance, internal structure, and phenotype estimates can be inferred from the chosen measurements and actions. A single top view cannot observe an underside; passive stereo may not observe depth on textureless surfaces; RGB cannot observe internal chalkiness or embryo cavities; and overlapping kernels may make individual geometry unobservable without singulation, additional views, or CT [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7706343), [[7]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12284-020-00429-w), [[31]](https://limbs.lcsr.jhu.edu/wp-content/uploads/2017/09/kunapareddy_acc2018_final.pdf).",
        "Controllability asks whether available actuators can place the system into information-rich sensing states. A turntable, robot arm, conveyor stop-start control, illumination array, autofocus, exposure controller, and sensor scheduler make the imaging process controllable. If the platform cannot separate overlapping kernels, rotate samples, change light, or re-scan uncertain samples, some target states remain practically uncontrollable even if algorithms are sophisticated [[32]](https://www.mathworks.com/videos/state-space-part-3-a-conceptual-approach-to-controllability-and-observability-1548327460100.html), [[50]](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/14/2/431).",
        "Kalman filtering and Bayesian estimation are appropriate for online pose tracking, calibration drift estimation, multi-view fusion, sensor scheduling, and trait uncertainty. Active sensing literature describes uncertainty criteria such as determinant, trace, weighted trace, maximum eigenvalue, entropy, entropy change, and KL divergence of covariance or probability distributions [[28]](https://www.dcsc.tudelft.nl/~sc4081/2018/assign/pap/andrea_ASR_paper.pdf). For grain traits, weighted-trace criteria are practical because length, width, thickness, volume, and defect area have different units and tolerances.",
        "Optimal control selects view, lighting, scan, and sensor actions to reduce expected uncertainty per unit cost. Model predictive control is useful because it repeatedly optimizes a finite-horizon action sequence under constraints such as field of view, focus, maximum acceleration, exposure, throughput, and safety [[34]](https://kth.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1479331/FULLTEXT01.pdf). Deep visual-servoing MPC research shows how predictive control can use visual features or optical flow to command camera motion, although agricultural deployment requires robust calibration and latency management [[35]](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v155/katara21a/katara21a.pdf).",
        "Robust and adaptive control address uncertainty in sensor calibration, vibration, lighting, grain pose, reflectance, and conveyor dynamics. Agricultural robotic visual-servoing literature distinguishes open-loop systems, which may accumulate positioning errors, from closed-loop systems that use continuous image feedback, and argues that stability analysis matters for reliable agricultural automation [[36]](https://nimss.org/projects/view/mrp/outline/18868).",
        "Active vision and next-best-view planning are the most direct control-theoretic tools for 3D reconstruction. Plant active vision research showed that automatically controlling camera viewpoints can improve 3D reconstruction relative to fixed-camera methods and reduce redundant images [[22]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6181042). NBV methods use probabilistic occupancy maps, entropy, expected information gain, visibility of unobserved surfaces, and movement cost to choose the next sensor pose [[37]](https://rpg.ifi.uzh.ch/docs/ICRA16_Isler.pdf). Closed-loop NBV methods can update a TSDF map and stop when task predictions stabilize or information gain falls below a threshold [[38]](https://jenjenchung.github.io/anthropomorphic/Papers/Breyer2022closed.pdf).",
        "In grain applications, the practical controller should implement four feedback loops: mechanical feedback for turntable or conveyor speed and vibration; optical feedback for exposure, focus, and illumination; estimation feedback for pose, calibration, and reconstruction uncertainty; and decision feedback for re-scan, reject, CT-reference sampling, or sorting.",
        "Control theory should not be over-applied. If a fixed structured-light chamber already meets required MAPE and throughput for singulated, uniform wheat kernels, a complex MPC controller may add maintenance and latency without measurable gain. The value of control increases when the sample or environment is variable enough that fixed acquisition either over-samples easy cases or under-samples hard cases [[28]](https://www.dcsc.tudelft.nl/~sc4081/2018/assign/pap/andrea_ASR_paper.pdf), [[36]](https://nimss.org/projects/view/mrp/outline/18868), [[37]](https://rpg.ifi.uzh.ch/docs/ICRA16_Isler.pdf)."

      "heading": "## 8. Other Relevant Theoretical Methods and Models",
      "content": [
        "Projective and multi-view geometry provide the measurement model for RGB, stereo, SfM, visual hull, NeRF, and 3DGS pipelines. The camera projection x ~ K[R|t]X links 3D points to pixels; stereo depth depends on disparity; and bundle adjustment refines camera parameters and 3D points by minimizing reprojection error [[33]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4523375/).       "heading": "## 14. Analysis Methods, Mechanisms, Causes, Implications, Debates, and Open Problems",
      "content": [
        "Error propagation mechanism: Pixel noise, exposure saturation, or poor feature localization causes depth or triangulation uncertainty; calibration drift creates systematic scale and pose bias; segmentation uncertainty shifts boundaries; registration error creates double surfaces or blurred meshes; incomplete views bias surface area and volume; and trait errors propagate into downstream GWAS, grading, or sorting decisions [[33]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4523375/), [[43]](https://cris.unibo.it/retrieve/handle/11585/1036507/df818831-72f1-4634-b91c-d63d8bf0b123/applsci-15-10985-1.pdf), [[81]](https://tsapps.nist.gov/publication/get_pdf.cfm), [[123]](https://www.nationalacademies.org/read/13395/chapter/3).",
        "Occlusion and overlap are fundamental causes of failure because unobserved surfaces are not measurable without additional views, mechanical separation, shape priors, or CT. GrainSet identifies pileup and occlusion from heterogeneous kernel shapes as a key acquisition challenge, and the legume laser study excluded overlapping and pasting seeds, limiting generalization [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10632488), [[50]](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/14/2/431).",
        "Reflectance and radiometry cause failures because specular, translucent, wet, or glossy surfaces violate assumptions in passive stereo, photometric stereo, and structured light. Ambient light can saturate structured-light patterns, and RGB-D fill rate varies strongly with lighting, distance, and target material [[81]](https://tsapps.nist.gov/publication/get_pdf.cfm), [[82]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6308665/).",
        "Neural methods introduce a different mechanism: learned priors infer hidden geometry from training distributions. This can reduce views and improve throughput, but it can also hallucinate plausible shapes for broken, rare, or out-of-distribution kernels. The wheat seed reconstruction study explicitly benefited from wheat seeds being simple, mostly convex, and approximately star-shaped [[85]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2023W/CVPPA/papers/Cherepashkin_Deep_Learning_Based_3d_Reconstruction_for_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_Seeds_ICCVW_2023_paper.pdf).",
        "Symmetry assumptions are a known boundary condition. The legume scanning workflow relied on approximately spherical or ellipsoidal, nearly symmetrical seeds and noted limitations for irregular shapes; therefore symmetry-based completion should not be used uncritically for broken, shriveled, diseased, or asymmetric kernels when those irregularities are themselves target phenotypes [[50]](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/14/2/431).",
        "The neural-versus-classical debate should be resolved by intended use. Classical structured light, laser scanning, CT, and volume carving are preferable when traceable metrology and interpretable error budgets are required. Few-view deep reconstruction, NeRF, and 3DGS are preferable as throughput accelerators only when validation shows that learned priors do not bias target traits across cultivars, damage classes, moisture states, and devices [[2]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2023W/CVPPA/html/Cherepashkin_Deep_Learning_Based_3d_Reconstruction_for_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_Seeds_ICCVW_2023_paper.html), [[14]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC13109325), [[16]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2025W/V4A/html/Zhang_Wheat3DGS_In-field_3D_Reconstruction_Instance_Segmentation_and_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_CVPRW_2025_paper.html), [[54]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/gam/jagris/v15y2025i22p2329-d1790563.html), [[85]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2023W/CVPPA/papers/Cherepashkin_Deep_Learning_Based_3d_Reconstruction_for_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_Seeds_ICCVW_2023_paper.pdf).",
        "Open problems include standardized multi-crop 3D grain benchmarks, uncertainty-aware neural reconstruction, real-time high-throughput 3D metrology, robust segmentation of touching and damaged grains, validation of 3DGS and NeRF at kernel scale, calibration transfer across devices, closed-loop active sensing under industrial throughput constraints, and linking geometric traits to biologically or commercially meaningful outcomes [[14]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC13109325), [[50]](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/14/2/431), [[85]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2023W/CVPPA/papers/Cherepashkin_Deep_Learning_Based_3d_Reconstruction_for_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_Seeds_ICCVW_2023_paper.pdf), [[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC13109328)."
      ]
    },
    {
      "heading": "## 15. Implementation Considerations for Agricultural Engineering Applications",
      "content": [
        "For breeding laboratories, prioritize repeatability, metadata integrity, batch automation, and compatibility with genotype and environment records. Seed-lot storage, moisture, harvest timing, and handling should be recorded because these factors can affect size, color, density, reflectance, and quality proxies [[10]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2025.1735309/full), [[111]](https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4395/4/2/242).",
        "For seed-quality facilities, prioritize throughput, robustness, cleaning, calibration, operator safety, defect detection, and auditability. Damaged and unsound grain categories require retaining hard cases rather than filtering them as outliers, because those cases are often the target of inspection [[89]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10632488/).",
        "For high-throughput conveyor systems, mechanical design is as important as reconstruction. Singulation, anti-static control, belt speed regulation, global-shutter cameras, strobe synchronization, dust management, and automatic reject/re-scan loops are needed to prevent motion blur, overlap, and contamination.",
        "For CT or X-ray systems, include radiation safety, shielding, interlocks, operator training, scan-dose documentation, and clear justification for internal-trait value relative to cost and throughput. CT is best used as a reference modality, internal-trait research tool, or validation subset unless the facility has industrial CT throughput and safety infrastructure [[6]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.601475/full), [[27]](https://www.seedtest.org/api/rm/XUV423T2XHYZ84Z/ista-congress-presentation-phenolytics-2025-for-we.pdf).",
        "For software implementation, store raw data, calibration files, preprocessing parameters, masks, point clouds, meshes, CT slices, uncertainty values, trait definitions, units, software versions, model versions, and validation status. Model-based traceability helps connect requirements, design elements, analysis metrics, and verification evidence [[130]](https://www.sei.cmu.edu/blog/requirements-in-model-based-systems-engineering-mbse), [[132]](https://www.designreview.byu.edu/collections/what-is-systems-design-part-3-the-case-for-model-based-systems-engineering).",
        "For deployment governance, every reported trait should carry its provenance: sensor, acquisition settings, calibration file, reconstruction algorithm, segmentation model, trait formula, validation reference, and uncertainty estimate. This is especially important when data are reused for GWAS, breeding decisions, quality grading, or model training because hidden changes in acquisition or processing can appear as biological differences [[10]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2025.1735309/full), [[123]](https://www.nationalacademies.org/read/13395/chapter/3), [[130]](https://www.sei.cmu.edu/blog/requirements-in-model-based-systems-engineering-mbse)."
      ]
    },
    {
      "heading": "## 16. Risks, Limitations, and Uncertainties",
      "content": [
        "Evidence limitation: Many grain studies are crop-specific, lab-specific, sensor-specific, and condition-specific. A 2025 survey states that plant 3D reconstruction lacks unified public datasets and benchmarks, with many studies relying on proprietary or small-scale datasets under specific crop and environmental conditions [[14]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC13109325).",
        "Ground-truth limitation: Calipers cannot validate true surface area, volume, ventral sulcus geometry, or internal structure; CT and volume carving have their own segmentation and reconstruction uncertainties; and volume-carving ground truth in the wheat seed challenge required outlier filtering and depends on view count [[6]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.601475/full), [[85]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2023W/CVPPA/papers/Cherepashkin_Deep_Learning_Based_3d_Reconstruction_for_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_Seeds_ICCVW_2023_paper.pdf).",
        "Deployment limitation: Field, warehouse, and conveyor environments introduce lighting, dust, vibration, moisture, and domain-shift problems that are often absent from lab datasets. Controlled indoor results should not be assumed to generalize without deployment-specific validation [[48]](https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4395/15/11/2518), [[82]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6308665/), [[89]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10632488/).",
        "Control limitation: Modern control theory requires an adequate model, observability, actuation authority, and reliable online measurements. A poorly calibrated or delayed feedback loop can increase blur, oscillation, or re-scan burden rather than improving phenotyping. Modern control can also be less intuitive and less robust if unmodeled dynamics and noise are ignored [[116]](https://lewisgroup.uta.edu/history.htm).",
        "Economic limitation: LiDAR, CT, micro-CT, XRM, hyperspectral cameras, robotic arms, and industrial scanners may be cost-prohibitive for some programs. Low-cost RGB systems improve access but may not meet submillimeter or internal-trait requirements [[6]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.601475/full), [[49]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC13030611), [[57]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4279472).",
        "Data limitation: The strongest evidence often uses controlled singulation and selected crops. Systems intended for bulk lots, damaged kernels, touching grains, dusty samples, or industrial speed must test those conditions directly rather than extrapolating from clean laboratory trays [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10632488), [[50]](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/14/2/431), [[89]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10632488/).",
        "Safety and compliance limitation: X-ray and CT workflows require radiation-safety engineering and institutional procedures. This report identifies the need for shielding, interlocks, training, and dose documentation but does not provide jurisdiction-specific regulatory thresholds because those provisions were not present in the evidence base [[6]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.601475/full), [[27]](https://www.seedtest.org/api/rm/XUV423T2XHYZ84Z/ista-congress-presentation-phenolytics-2025-for-we.pdf)."
      ]
    },
    {
      "heading": "## 17. Recommendations and Future Outlook",
      "content": [
        "Recommendation 1: Build a hybrid reference-and-throughput system. Use structured light or laser triangulation for routine external 3D traits, RGB/MSI for color and quality proxies, and CT or micro-CT for internal traits and validation subsets [[1]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2022.840908/full), [[6]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.601475/full), [[10]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2025.1735309/full), [[50]](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/14/2/431).",
        "Recommendation 2: Make uncertainty a first-class output. Every trait should include a measurement value, unit, acquisition metadata, reconstruction completeness, segmentation confidence, and uncertainty or repeatability estimate. Re-scan rules should be based on trait-level uncertainty rather than generic image-quality heuristics [[28]](https://www.dcsc.tudelft.nl/~sc4081/2018/assign/pap/andrea_ASR_paper.pdf), [[123]](https://www.nationalacademies.org/read/13395/chapter/3).",
        "Recommendation 3: Use control theory pragmatically. Start with deterministic fixed-view acquisition when samples are simple and trait error is already within tolerance. Add feedback control for exposure, illumination, view selection, conveyor speed, vibration, and re-scan decisions where it measurably improves observability, robustness, or throughput [[22]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6181042), [[36]](https://nimss.org/projects/view/mrp/outline/18868), [[37]](https://rpg.ifi.uzh.ch/docs/ICRA16_Isler.pdf).",
        "Recommendation 4: Validate neural methods as accelerators, not black boxes. Use 3DGS, NeRF, and few-view deep reconstruction for speed only after cross-cultivar, cross-condition, and cross-sensor validation. Include out-of-distribution detection and uncertainty-triggered fallback to structured light or CT [[2]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2023W/CVPPA/html/Cherepashkin_Deep_Learning_Based_3d_Reconstruction_for_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_Seeds_ICCVW_2023_paper.html), [[16]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2025W/V4A/html/Zhang_Wheat3DGS_In-field_3D_Reconstruction_Instance_Segmentation_and_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_CVPRW_2025_paper.html), [[54]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/gam/jagris/v15y2025i22p2329-d1790563.html), [[93]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.15981v1).",
        "Recommendation 5: Create open multi-crop 3D grain benchmarks. A benchmark should include wheat, rice, maize, soybean, sorghum, intact and damaged kernels, multiple moisture states, CT or high-accuracy surface references, raw images, calibration files, masks, point clouds, meshes, trait measurements, and hidden test sets [[14]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC13109325), [[29]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41597-026-07207-1), [[85]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2023W/CVPPA/papers/Cherepashkin_Deep_Learning_Based_3d_Reconstruction_for_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_Seeds_ICCVW_2023_paper.pdf).",
        "Recommendation 6: Treat sample handling as part of the sensing system. Singulation, stable motion, anti-static handling, dust control, and background selection should be designed and validated alongside algorithms because overlap and hidden surfaces are dominant causal mechanisms of error [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10632488), [[50]](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/14/2/431).",
        "Future outlook: The most credible near-term path is hybrid intelligence, not replacement of metrology by neural rendering. Classical geometry and calibrated sensors should provide the measurement backbone; neural methods should reduce acquisition burden, improve segmentation, or complete low-risk shapes; and control theory should decide when those estimates are sufficiently observable or when additional sensing is required [[14]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC13109325), [[49]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC13030611), [[54]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/gam/jagris/v15y2025i22p2329-d1790563.html)."
      ]
    },
    {
      "heading": "## 18. Conclusion",
      "content": [
        "The best research design for crop-grain 3D reconstruction and phenotypic analysis is a closed-loop, uncertainty-aware, modular system. Modern control theory supplies the framework for representing states, inputs, outputs, disturbances, objectives, constraints, observability, estimation, and feedback decisions. Geometry, radiometry, CT physics, computational geometry, machine learning, uncertainty quantification, and systems engineering supply the specialized models and evidence needed for reconstruction, trait extraction, validation, and deployment.",
        "For near-term agricultural engineering applications, structured light and laser triangulation are the most practical primary external 3D sensors; CT and micro-CT are the strongest reference and internal-trait modalities; RGB/MSI systems are essential for high-throughput surface and quality screening; and neural methods such as few-view reconstruction, NeRF, and 3DGS are promising accelerators that require stronger grain-scale validation. The most important design principle is to connect modeling, analysis, and design through traceability: every sensor choice, algorithm, controller, trait, validation metric, and deployment decision should be tied to an explicit system objective and uncertainty budget."
      ]
    }

          "weaknesses": "Line-of-sight only; affected by reflectance, ambient light, occlusion, and registration.",

          "strengths": "Robust to specular reflections and low contrast in controlled wheat seed data [[85]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2023W/CVPPA/papers/Cherepashkin_Deep_Learning_Based_3d_Reconstruction_for_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_Seeds_ICCVW_2023_paper.pdf).",
          "weaknesses": "Requires masks and many views for high accuracy; concavities and internal traits are not recovered.",

          "validation_priority": "Quantify sensitivity to mask errors and view count."
        },

          "algorithm": "CT voxel reconstruction and segmentation",
          "inputs": "X-ray projections or CT slices.",
          "strengths": "Internal and external 3D traits; strong reference modality [[3]](https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4395/13/4/1078), [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7706343), [[6]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.601475/full).",
          "weaknesses": "Cost, scan time, radiation safety, segmentation uncertainty.",
          "best_use": "Internal anatomy, validation, chalkiness, embryo/endosperm/cavity studies.",

    "table_4_theory_comparison": {
      "heading": "### Mandatory Table: Modern Control Theory Compared with Complementary Frameworks",
      "rows": [
        {
          "framework": "Modern control theory",
          "role": "Controls sensing actions and actuator states using state-space models, feedback, estimation, MPC, robust control, and active vision.",
          "strength": "Closes the loop between uncertainty and acquisition decisions.",
          "limitation": "Requires adequate models, observability, controllability, and stable feedback under noise [[116]](https://lewisgroup.uta.edu/history.htm).",
          "grain_design_implication": "Use it to select view, illumination, speed, and re-scan actions, not to replace reconstruction physics."
        },
        {
          "framework": "Projective and multi-view geometry",
          "role": "Maps images to 3D structure through projection, triangulation, epipolar constraints, and bundle adjustment.",
          "strength": "Foundational for RGB, stereo, SfM, NeRF, and 3DGS.",
          "limitation": "Does not itself decide which views to acquire or quantify biological usefulness [[33]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4523375/).",
          "grain_design_implication": "Use it for calibration, camera-pose estimation, and triangulation; pair with control for view planning."
        },
        {
          "framework": "Radiometry and reflectance",
          "role": "Models light, sensor response, exposure, spectral reflectance, shadows, and highlights.",
          "strength": "Essential for color, MSI, HSI, defect detection, and photometric reconstruction.",
          "limitation": "Confounded by geometry and surface properties without calibration and fusion [[43]](https://cris.unibo.it/retrieve/handle/11585/1036507/df818831-72f1-4634-b91c-d63d8bf0b123/applsci-15-10985-1.pdf).",
          "grain_design_implication": "Use controlled illumination, calibration panels, and glare detection before comparing color or spectral traits."
        },
        {
          "framework": "Bayesian inference and UQ",
          "role": "Quantifies posterior uncertainty, confidence intervals, error propagation, and model credibility.",
          "strength": "Supports re-scan decisions and trait-level uncertainty.",
          "limitation": "Requires realistic uncertainty models and validation data [[123]](https://www.nationalacademies.org/read/13395/chapter/3).",
          "grain_design_implication": "Report confidence or repeatability with every trait and use uncertainty to trigger re-scan."
        },
        {
          "framework": "Machine learning and deep learning",
          "role": "Segments grains, reconstructs few-view shapes, predicts traits, and fuses modalities.",
          "strength": "High throughput and adaptability when training data are representative.",
          "limitation": "Domain shift, annotation burden, and hidden bias remain major risks [[9]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42003-020-0905-5), [[85]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2023W/CVPPA/papers/Cherepashkin_Deep_Learning_Based_3d_Reconstruction_for_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_Seeds_ICCVW_2023_paper.pdf), [[101]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC13109328).",
          "grain_design_implication": "Use ML as a validated accelerator or segmentation module, with fallback to calibrated sensing when uncertainty is high."
        },
        {
          "framework": "Systems engineering and MBSE",
          "role": "Connects requirements, architecture, models, analysis, validation, and deployment through traceability.",
          "strength": "Improves auditability, defect prevention, and change impact analysis.",
          "limitation": "Requires disciplined process and skilled model-based engineering practice [[121]](https://www.incose.org/group/mbse-initiative), [[134]](https://www.incose.org/docs/default-source/enchantment/161109-carrolled-howismodel-basedsystemsengineeringjustified-researchreport.pdf).",
          "grain_design_implication": "Maintain requirement-to-test traceability for each trait, sensor, algorithm, and controller."
        }
      ]
    },

    "table_6_key_findings_evidence_quality_implications": {
      "heading": "### Mandatory Table: Key Findings, Evidence Quality, and Implications",
      "rows": [
        {
          "finding": "Structured light and laser triangulation are the best-supported primary external 3D modalities for controlled grain phenotyping.",
          "evidence_quality": "High for external laboratory morphology because multiple grain or seed studies report concrete MAPE, reconstruction error, and throughput values.",
          "evidence": "Wheat structured light MAPE 1.83% length, 1.86% width, 2.19% thickness; cereal structured light errors 2.07%, 0.97%, 1.13%; legume laser reconstruction error 0.014 mm [[1]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2022.840908/full), [[50]](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/14/2/431), [[91]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8873360).",
          "implication": "Use these modalities as the baseline measurement backbone for external 3D traits."
        },
        {
          "finding": "CT and micro-CT are necessary when internal traits are central.",
          "evidence_quality": "High for internal morphology but lower for high-throughput deployment.",
          "evidence": "Rice CT R² = 0.980 for grain number and 0.960 for grain length; maize CT extracted internal kernel traits and supported GWAS [[3]](https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4395/13/4/1078), [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7706343).",
          "implication": "Use CT for embryo, endosperm, cavity, chalkiness, and reference subsets rather than as the default sensor for routine external screening."
        },
        {
          "finding": "2D and MSI systems remain valuable but cannot replace true 3D for volumetric or hidden traits.",
          "evidence_quality": "High for screening throughput; limited for 3D observability.",
          "evidence": "Wheat MSI processed more than 70,000 seeds at about 3,000 seeds/hour with R² ≥ 0.949, but 2D/spectral imaging does not directly observe thickness, volume, internal cavities, or hidden surfaces [[1]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2022.840908/full), [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7706343), [[6]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2020.601475/full), [[10]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2025.1735309/full).",
          "implication": "Fuse RGB/MSI with 3D sensing when both quality proxies and geometry are required."
        },
        {
          "finding": "Neural reconstruction can reduce image count and acquisition time but remains validation-sensitive.",
          "evidence_quality": "Moderate for controlled wheat seed few-view reconstruction; emerging for multi-crop 3DGS; weaker for field-grain metrology.",
          "evidence": "Wheat few-view learning reduced imaging time 10× with around 2% relative errors, while Wheat3DGS field wheat-head volume error was 40.2% MAPE [[2]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2023W/CVPPA/html/Cherepashkin_Deep_Learning_Based_3d_Reconstruction_for_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_Seeds_ICCVW_2023_paper.html), [[16]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2025W/V4A/html/Zhang_Wheat3DGS_In-field_3D_Reconstruction_Instance_Segmentation_and_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_CVPRW_2025_paper.html), [[54]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/gam/jagris/v15y2025i22p2329-d1790563.html), [[85]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2023W/CVPPA/papers/Cherepashkin_Deep_Learning_Based_3d_Reconstruction_for_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_Seeds_ICCVW_2023_paper.pdf).",
          "implication": "Deploy neural methods as accelerators with uncertainty flags and fallback to calibrated sensing."
        },
        {
          "finding": "Closed-loop sensing is most valuable when observability varies across samples.",
          "evidence_quality": "Strong theoretical basis and plant active-vision evidence; grain-specific closed-loop evidence remains limited.",
          "evidence": "Active sensing formalizes uncertainty and action selection; plant active vision improved reconstruction compared with fixed views; grain studies show acquisition geometry affects accuracy [[1]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2022.840908/full), [[22]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6181042), [[28]](https://www.dcsc.tudelft.nl/~sc4081/2018/assign/pap/andrea_ASR_paper.pdf), [[37]](https://rpg.ifi.uzh.ch/docs/ICRA16_Isler.pdf).",
          "implication": "Implement feedback first for exposure, illumination, re-scan, and next-best-view decisions where fixed acquisition fails."
        },
        {
          "finding": "Benchmarks and validation datasets are the major bottleneck for generalizable grain-scale 3D AI.",
          "evidence_quality": "Moderate to high as a cross-study limitation.",
          "evidence": "The wheat seed challenge has 2,964 filtered seeds; the 2026 wheat CT dataset has 100 kernels; reviews report lack of unified public datasets and benchmarks [[14]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC13109325), [[29]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41597-026-07207-1), [[85]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/ICCV2023W/CVPPA/papers/Cherepashkin_Deep_Learning_Based_3d_Reconstruction_for_Phenotyping_of_Wheat_Seeds_ICCVW_2023_paper.pdf).",
          "implication": "Research groups should publish raw data, calibration, masks, point clouds, meshes, CT references, and hidden test sets."
        }
      ]
